content:
Please describe in detail what, exactly, happens when you "can not log in" - quote any error messages you see verbatim. Screen shots will suffice. Can you view your email on your providers web mail interface? Are all the messages you tried to delete still there or are they gone?

When a program crashes unexpectedly it often continues to run invisibly in the background. There are various ways to kill those phantom processes, but the easiest is to simply reboot the computer. Have you done that? If so and it's still misbehaving there are other measures to take, but start with that one.

content: Go to TB menu > Account Settings > account > Server Settings and make note of the User Name. Then scroll down to Outgoing Server, where there should be an entry for each of the three accounts. Make sure each of the three accounts' User Names are the same as their respective Server Settings User Names.

content: That is a message from Google, not from Thunderbird. Google is asking if it's OK for a third-party program (Thunderbird in this case) to access your Google account. The notifications will stop once you either Accept, or remove the Thunderbird account that is trying to access the Google account. In other words, it's under your control.

content:
peter.f.hjort said
To be really useful to me I need Thunderbird to be able to read my Outlook pst-files. Yet it can not do so. There is absolutely not process you can undertake to make Thunderbird read a PST file. It has no idea that it is even a mail file.
For years my practice has been to move emails from the ost-file to the pst-file too limit the size of the ost-file. Both files are localized on my computer but only the emails in the ost-file are in my gmail-account. So the method helps me limit the size of my gmail-account but still gives me total access to my email-history.

Except now you have encountered the primary reason computer professionals have made recommendations against using proprietary file formats to store archives for at least the last 30 years, probably longer., but that is how far back I remember it happening. Only the application that created them can actually use them without some sort of conversion.

Without a similar possibility in Thunderbird it's difficult for me to leave Outlook, which I really want to. Can you help me?

Thunderbird can import mail from the default outlook program installed on the system when it is installed. So if the outlook application sees the mail to export it to Thunderbird then Thunderbird will get it. If outlook does not offer it, then Thunderbird will not get the email. The import uses dynamic data exchange and relies on Outlook to offer the mail to be imported. So if you have some sort of archive you have to load, it may well be that the import will not see those emails at all.

My understanding is it is only the "default" file that is imported, but as I have not used outlook for around 25 years, I have no real recent experience.

You may want to invest in some Third party software to convert your proprietary files into open file formats that Thunderbird understands like EML and MBOX. Use a search engine to search PST to MBOX converter. My search even showed some in the Microsoft store. I understand that there are a lot of vendors offering such software as you are not alone in wanting to access your outlook files in software other than Outlook.
creator: MattAuSupport
